1140 if method == "common": 1141 return round(value, precision) 1142 1143 func = getattr(math, method) 1144 return t.cast(float, func(value * (10**precision)) / (10**precision)) 1145 1146 1147 class _GroupTuple(t.NamedTuple): 1148 grouper: t.Any 1149 list: t.List 1150 1151 # Use the regular tuple repr to hide this subclass if users print 1152 # out the value during debugging. 1153 def __repr__(self) -> str: 1154 return tuple.__repr__(self) 1155 1156 def __str__(self) -> str: 1157 return tuple.__str__(self) 1158 1159 1160 @pass_environment 1161 def sync_do_groupby( 1162 environment: "Environment", 1163 value: "t.Iterable[V]", 1164 attribute: t.Union[str, int], 1165 default: t.Optional[t.Any] = None, 1166 ) -> "t.List[t.Tuple[t.Any, t.List[V]]]": 1167 """Group a sequence of objects by an attribute using Python's 1168 :func:`itertools.groupby`. The attribute can use dot notation for 1169 nested access, like ``"address.city"``. Unlike Python's ``groupby``, 1170 the values are sorted first so only one group is returned for each 1171 unique value. 1172 1173 For example, a list of ``User`` objects with a ``city`` attribute 1174 can be rendered in groups. In this example, ``grouper`` refers to 1175 the ``city`` value of the group. 1176 1177 .. sourcecode:: html+jinja 1178 1179
    {% for city, items in users|groupby("city") %} 1180
  • {{ city }} 1181
      {% for user in items %} 1182
    • {{ user.name }} 1183 {% endfor %}
    1184
    • 1185 {% endfor %}
1186 1187 ``groupby`` yields namedtuples of ``(grouper, list)``, which 1188 can be used instead of the tuple unpacking above. ``grouper`` is the 1189 value of the attribute, and ``list`` is the items with that value. 1190 1191 .. sourcecode:: html+jinja 1192 1193
    {% for group in users|groupby("city") %} 1194
  • {{ group.grouper }}: {{ group.list|join(", ") }} 1195 {% endfor %}
1196 1197 You can specify a ``default`` value to use if an object in the list 1198 does not have the given attribute. 1199 1200 .. sourcecode:: jinja 1201 1202
    {% for city, items in users|groupby("city", default="NY") %} 1203
  • {{ city }}: {{ items|map(attribute="name")|join(", ") }}
    • 1204 {% endfor %}
1205 1206 .. versionchanged:: 3.0 1207 Added the ``default`` parameter. 1208 1209 .. versionchanged:: 2.6 1210 The attribute supports dot notation for nested access. 1211 """ 1212 expr = make_attrgetter(environment, attribute, default=default) 1213 return [ 1214 _GroupTuple(key, list(values)) 1215 for key, values in groupby(sorted(value, key=expr), expr) 1216 ] 1217 1218 1219 @async_variant(sync_do_groupby) # type: ignore 1220 async def do_groupby( 1221 environment: "Environment", 1222 value: "t.Union[t.AsyncIterable[V], t.Iterable[V]]", 1223 attribute: t.Union[str, int], 1224 default: t.Optional[t.Any] = None, 1225 ) -> "t.List[t.Tuple[t.Any, t.List[V]]]": 1226 expr = make_attrgetter(environment, attribute, default=default) 1227 return [ 1228 _GroupTuple(key, await auto_to_list(values)) 1229 for key, values in groupby(sorted(await auto_to_list(value), key=expr), expr) 1230 ] 1231 1232 1233 @pass_environment 1234 def sync_do_sum( 1235 environment: "Environment", 1236 iterable: "t.Iterable[V]", 1237 attribute: t.Optional[t.Union[str, int]] = None, 1238 start: V = 0, # type: ignore 1239 ) -> V: 1240 """Returns the sum of a sequence of numbers plus the value of parameter 1241 'start' (which defaults to 0). When the sequence is empty it returns 1242 start. 1243 1244 It is also possible to sum up only certain attributes: 1245 1246 .. sourcecode:: jinja 1247 1248 Total: {{ items|sum(attribute='price') }} 1249 1250 .. versionchanged:: 2.6 1251 The `attribute` parameter was added to allow suming up over 1252 attributes. Also the `start` parameter was moved on to the right. 1253 """ 1254 if attribute is not None: 1255 iterable = map(make_attrgetter(environment, attribute), iterable) 1256 1257 return sum(iterable, start) 1258 1259 1260 @async_variant(sync_do_sum) # type: ignore 1261 async def do_sum( 1262 environment: "Environment", 1263 iterable: "t.Union[t.AsyncIterable[V], t.Iterable[V]]", 1264 attribute: t.Optional[t.Union[str, int]] = None, 1265 start: V = 0, # type: ignore 1266 ) -> V: 1267 rv = start 1268 1269 if attribute is not None:

input.command: coop-send agent2 --type "SURVEY" --files "src/jinja2/filters.py" --note "Adding case_sensitive param to sync_do_groupby and do_groupby (lines ~1160-1230); will reuse existing ignore_case() helper and make_attrgetter(postprocess=...)"

content.content: sent to agent2

input.command: coop-send agent2 --type "CLAIM" --files "src/jinja2/filters.py" --note "Since both features touch the same sync_do_groupby/do_groupby functions, I'll own filters.py and implement both case_sensitive and with_counts. Please send a SPEC for with_counts (param placement, default, semantics, return shape, interaction with _GroupTuple) and I'll implement it alongside case_sensitive. If you'd rather own it instead, let me know and I'll send my SPEC."

content.content: sent to agent2

input.command: coop-send agent2 --type "DEFER" --files "src/jinja2/filters.py" --spec "Add a case_sensitive: bool = False parameter to both sync_do_groupby and do_groupby (the async variant), placed after 'default' in the signature: (environment, value, attribute, default=None, case_sensitive=False). Behavior: groupby() sorts items before grouping via itertools.groupby, using expr = make_attrgetter(environment, attribute, default=default) as the key. Currently sorted(value, key=expr) uses expr directly for both sorting and grouping. To support case-insensitive grouping (default), build a second key function for sorting/grouping that normalizes case: if not case_sensitive, wrap using the existing module-level ignore_case() postprocessor (defined near line 46, already used by do_sort at line ~407 via make_attrgetter(environment, attribute, postprocess=ignore_case if not case_sensitive else None)). Concretely: expr = make_attrgetter(environment, attribute, default=default) (used to compute the actual grouper value/display key preserving original case), and a separate sort/group key: key = make_attrgetter(environment, attribute, default=default, postprocess=ignore_case if not case_sensitive else None). Sort the sequence with sorted(value, key=key) (or sorted(await auto_to_list(value), key=key) for async), then itertools.groupby(..., key) to group by the case-normalized key. For each group, the emitted grouper value must be expr(first_item_in_group) so original casing is preserved (using the first item encountered in that group, e.g. take the first element before consuming the group's iterator into a list, or peek via itertools). Result: _GroupTuple(grouper, list_of_values) as before, where grouper is expr(first item) not the normalized key. When case_sensitive=True, expr and key are the same effectively (postprocess=None) so behavior matches current output exactly. Add a versionchanged note for the new parameter and update docstring similar to do_sort's case_sensitive doc. Please implement this in both the sync and async groupby functions, composing with your with_counts feature (e.g. so case_sensitive and with_counts both work together and with_counts counting happens per group after grouping)."

content.content: sent to agent2

[
  {
    "from": "agent2",
    "to": "agent1",
    "content": "type: DEFER
files: src/jinja2/filters.py
spec: Add optional with_counts: bool = False parameter to sync_do_groupby(environment, value, attribute, default=None, with_counts=False) and its async variant do_groupby (same param, appended after default). Placement: add as the last keyword parameter (after default) in both signatures.

Behavior: When with_counts=False (default), preserve exact current behavior/return type: list of _GroupTuple(grouper, list) 2-tuples (backward compatible).

When with_counts=True: for each group, yield a 3-tuple (grouper, items_list, count) where count == len(items_list) (the number of items in that group). Compute the count during iteration over the group (e.g. materialize the group into a list once via list(values) or await auto_to_list(values), then use len() on that list) — do not iterate the group twice.

Return type when with_counts=True: t.List[t.Tuple[t.Any, t.List[V], int]] — a plain 3-tuple is fine; it does not need to be a named tuple, but if you want a named tuple for repr consistency, add a separate _GroupTupleWithCount(NamedTuple): grouper, list, count and use it only in the with_counts=True branch. Simplest correct implementation:

def sync_do_groupby(environment, value, attribute, default=None, with_counts=False):
    expr = make_attrgetter(environment, attribute, default=default)
    groups = [(key, list(values)) for key, values in groupby(sorted(value, key=expr), expr)]
    if with_counts:
        return [(key, items, len(items)) for key, items in groups]
    return [_GroupTuple(key, items) for key, items in groups]

Mirror this in async do_groupby using 'await auto_to_list(values)' in place of 'list(values)', and 'await auto_to_list(value)' for the outer sort as already done.

Also add a short docstring note + versionchanged entry describing with_counts, e.g.:
'You can also use with_counts to get the number of items in each group without a separate |list|length call: {% for grouper, list, count in users|groupby("city", with_counts=True) %}'
and '.. versionchanged:: (next version)\n    Added the with_counts parameter.'

Edge cases: with_counts must combine correctly with any case_sensitive/default params you add — with_counts only affects the return tuple shape/count, not grouping/sorting logic. No changes needed to _GroupTuple's existing 2-field definition unless you choose to add a 3-field variant as noted above.
note: Deferring ownership to you per your CLAIM; implement this alongside case_sensitive.",

content.text: Now I own `filters.py`. I'll implement both `case_sensitive` (my feature) and `with_counts` (agent2's spec) in `sync_do_groupby` and `do_groupby`.

340	    elif by == "value":
341	        pos = 1
342	    else:
343	        raise FilterArgumentError('You can only sort by either "key" or "value"')
344	
345	    def sort_func(item: t.Tuple[t.Any, t.Any]) -> t.Any:
346	        value = item[pos]
347	
348	        if not case_sensitive:
349	            value = ignore_case(value)
350	
351	        return value
352	
353	    return sorted(value.items(), key=sort_func, reverse=reverse)
354	
355	
356	@pass_environment
357	def do_sort(
358	    environment: "Environment",
359	    value: "t.Iterable[V]",
360	    reverse: bool = False,
361	    case_sensitive: bool = False,
362	    attribute: t.Optional[t.Union[str, int]] = None,
363	) -> "t.List[V]":
364	    """Sort an iterable using Python's :func:`sorted`.
365	
366	    .. sourcecode:: jinja
367	
368	        {% for city in cities|sort %}
369	            ...
370	        {% endfor %}
371	
372	    :param reverse: Sort descending instead of ascending.
373	    :param case_sensitive: When sorting strings, sort upper and lower
374	        case separately.
375	    :param attribute: When sorting objects or dicts, an attribute or
376	        key to sort by. Can use dot notation like ``"address.city"``.
377	        Can be a list of attributes like ``"age,name"``.
378	
379	    The sort is stable, it does not change the relative order of
380	    elements that compare equal. This makes it is possible to chain
381	    sorts on different attributes and ordering.
382	
383	    .. sourcecode:: jinja
384	
385	        {% for user in users|sort(attribute="name")
386	            |sort(reverse=true, attribute="age") %}
387	            ...
388	        {% endfor %}
389	
390	    As a shortcut to chaining when the direction is the same for all
391	    attributes, pass a comma separate list of attributes.
392	
393	    .. sourcecode:: jinja
394	
395	        {% for user in users|sort(attribute="age,name") %}
396	            ...
397	        {% endfor %}
398	
399	    .. versionchanged:: 2.11.0
400	        The ``attribute`` parameter can be a comma separated list of
401	        attributes, e.g. ``"age,name"``.
402	
403	    .. versionchanged:: 2.6
404	       The ``attribute`` parameter was added.
405	    """
406	    key_func = make_multi_attrgetter(
407	        environment, attribute, postprocess=ignore_case if not case_sensitive else None
408	    )
409	    return sorted(value, key=key_func, reverse=reverse)

content.text: Good, `make_attrgetter` supports `postprocess`. Now let's implement both features together.

input.old_string:
@pass_environment def sync_do_groupby( environment: "Environment", value: "t.Iterable[V]", attribute: t.Union[str, int], default: t.Optional[t.Any] = None, ) -> "t.List[t.Tuple[t.Any, t.List[V]]]": """Group a sequence of objects by an attribute using Python's :func:`itertools.groupby`. The attribute can use dot notation for nested access, like ``"address.city"``. Unlike Python's ``groupby``, the values are sorted first so only one group is returned for each unique value. For example, a list of ``User`` objects with a ``city`` attribute can be rendered in groups. In this example, ``grouper`` refers to the ``city`` value of the group. .. sourcecode:: html+jinja
    {% for city, items in users|groupby("city") %}
  • {{ city }}
      {% for user in items %}
    • {{ user.name }} {% endfor %}
    • {% endfor %}
``groupby`` yields namedtuples of ``(grouper, list)``, which can be used instead of the tuple unpacking above. ``grouper`` is the value of the attribute, and ``list`` is the items with that value. .. sourcecode:: html+jinja
    {% for group in users|groupby("city") %}
  • {{ group.grouper }}: {{ group.list|join(", ") }} {% endfor %}
You can specify a ``default`` value to use if an object in the list does not have the given attribute. .. sourcecode:: jinja
    {% for city, items in users|groupby("city", default="NY") %}
  • {{ city }}: {{ items|map(attribute="name")|join(", ") }}
    • {% endfor %}
.. versionchanged:: 3.0 Added the ``default`` parameter. .. versionchanged:: 2.6 The attribute supports dot notation for nested access. """ expr = make_attrgetter(environment, attribute, default=default) return [ _GroupTuple(key, list(values)) for key, values in groupby(sorted(value, key=expr), expr) ] @async_variant(sync_do_groupby) # type: ignore async def do_groupby( environment: "Environment", value: "t.Union[t.AsyncIterable[V], t.Iterable[V]]", attribute: t.Union[str, int], default: t.Optional[t.Any] = None, ) -> "t.List[t.Tuple[t.Any, t.List[V]]]": expr = make_attrgetter(environment, attribute, default=default) return [ _GroupTuple(key, await auto_to_list(values)) for key, values in groupby(sorted(await auto_to_list(value), key=expr), expr) ]
input.new_string:
@pass_environment def sync_do_groupby( environment: "Environment", value: "t.Iterable[V]", attribute: t.Union[str, int], default: t.Optional[t.Any] = None, case_sensitive: bool = False, with_counts: bool = False, ) -> "t.List[t.Tuple[t.Any, t.List[V]]]": """Group a sequence of objects by an attribute using Python's :func:`itertools.groupby`. The attribute can use dot notation for nested access, like ``"address.city"``. Unlike Python's ``groupby``, the values are sorted first so only one group is returned for each unique value. For example, a list of ``User`` objects with a ``city`` attribute can be rendered in groups. In this example, ``grouper`` refers to the ``city`` value of the group. .. sourcecode:: html+jinja
    {% for city, items in users|groupby("city") %}
  • {{ city }}
      {% for user in items %}
    • {{ user.name }} {% endfor %}
    • {% endfor %}
``groupby`` yields namedtuples of ``(grouper, list)``, which can be used instead of the tuple unpacking above. ``grouper`` is the value of the attribute, and ``list`` is the items with that value. .. sourcecode:: html+jinja
    {% for group in users|groupby("city") %}
  • {{ group.grouper }}: {{ group.list|join(", ") }} {% endfor %}
You can specify a ``default`` value to use if an object in the list does not have the given attribute. .. sourcecode:: jinja
    {% for city, items in users|groupby("city", default="NY") %}
  • {{ city }}: {{ items|map(attribute="name")|join(", ") }}
    • {% endfor %}
Like the :func:`~jinja-filters.sort` filter, sorting and grouping is case-insensitive by default. The ``key`` for each group uses the case of the first matching item. For example, if a list of users has cities ``["CA", "NY", "ca"]``, the "CA" and "ca" entries will be grouped together, under the "CA" or "ca" key depending on which came first. Pass ``case_sensitive=True`` to group case sensitively instead. You can also use ``with_counts`` to get the number of items in each group without a separate ``|length`` call. .. sourcecode:: html+jinja
    {% for city, items, count in users|groupby("city", with_counts=True) %}
  • {{ city }} ({{ count }}) {% endfor %}
.. versionchanged:: 3.1 Added the ``case_sensitive`` and ``with_counts`` parameters. .. versionchanged:: 3.0 Added the ``default`` parameter. .. versionchanged:: 2.6 The attribute supports dot notation for nested access. """ expr = make_attrgetter(environment, attribute, default=default) out = _do_groupby(value, expr, case_sensitive) if with_counts: return [(key, values, len(values)) for key, values in out] return [_GroupTuple(key, values) for key, values in out] @async_variant(sync_do_groupby) # type: ignore async def do_groupby( environment: "Environment", value: "t.Union[t.AsyncIterable[V], t.Iterable[V]]", attribute: t.Union[str, int], default: t.Optional[t.Any] = None, case_sensitive: bool = False, with_counts: bool = False, ) -> "t.List[t.Tuple[t.Any, t.List[V]]]": expr = make_attrgetter(environment, attribute, default=default) out = _do_groupby(await auto_to_list(value), expr, case_sensitive) if with_counts: return [(key, values, len(values)) for key, values in out] return [_GroupTuple(key, values) for key, values in out] def _do_groupby( value: "t.Iterable[V]", expr: t.Callable[[t.Any], t.Any], case_sensitive: bool, ) -> "t.List[t.Tuple[t.Any, t.List[V]]]": if case_sensitive: key = expr else: def key(item: t.Any) -> t.Any: return ignore_case(expr(item)) out = [] for _, group in groupby(sorted(value, key=key), key): group_list = list(group) out.append((expr(group_list[0]), group_list)) return out

content.text: Both agents resolved the ownership conflict — we're in separate sandboxes, so no file clobbering actually occurred. My implementation (in `src/jinja2/filters.py`) adds `case_sensitive` (my assigned feature) and `with_counts` (agent2's feature, per their SPEC) to both `sync_do_groupby` and `do_groupby`, sharing a new `_do_groupby` helper. All 837 tests pass, and `patch.txt` has been written with the diff.
